playbook_entries.rule_title: Morning Send Window: Audit, Angle, Send (9–11 AM)
playbook_entries.guidance: Each morning between 9 and 11 AM, run 3–5 homepage audits on new target accounts before you send anything. Identify 1–2 specific problems — vague hero language, missing trust signals, weak CTA, slow decision path — and note the business consequence. Then draft your angle from that finding and send in the 9:30–11:00 AM window. Cold emails sent Tuesday–Thursday in this window outperform other windows by 25–40%. Send one-by-one, not in bulk. Personalisation must be visible: company name, specific issue, and one strength you noticed.
examples: Bad: 'Your website could use better design.' Good: 'Your paid traffic is strong but your hero doesn't give me a reason to act. That usually costs conversion on cold traffic.'
Bad: 'Your CTA could be stronger.' Good: 'Your CTA exists but isn't tied to an outcome. That hesitation typically costs form completions — a quick reframe usually lifts it 20–30%.'
Step 1: Visit homepage → identify the primary issue → note the business consequence. Step 2: Draft angle from the specific failure. Step 3: Send in the 9:30–11 AM window.
edge_cases: If you have no new accounts to audit, use the audit time to deepen angles on existing prospects that haven't replied in 3+ days.
If audit uncovers no clear issue, park the account and move to the next — forced messaging on weak findings produces low reply rates.
Monday mornings: review weekly metrics first (20 mins), then audit from 9:30 AM.

playbook_entries.guidance: Between 12 and 2 PM, triage all morning replies. Any positive or neutral reply must get a response within 2 hours. Speed-to-reply is a direct signal of professionalism — slow follow-up drops conversion from positive reply to booked call by 40–60%. Categorise each reply: positive (move to call booking), neutral/question (nurture with asset or answer), negative (log and close). For positive replies, send a calendar link in the response — don't ask 'when are you free?' Make the booking frictionless.
examples: Positive reply: 'Sounds interesting, tell me more.' → Respond: 'Happy to. Here's a quick overview of what I spotted + a 15-min slot to walk through it: [calendar link].'
Neutral reply: 'What do you mean by conversion gap?' → Respond with one specific data point from their site and offer the checklist asset.
Negative reply: 'Not interested right now.' → Log as objection type, note timing, and set a re-engagement reminder for 60 days.
edge_cases: If you're in back-to-back meetings 12–2 PM, set a 15-minute reply block at 11:45 AM and 3:00 PM as fallback.
Replies that arrive after 4 PM should be actioned first thing the next morning, not late in the day.
Never send calendar links without context — always pair the link with one specific reason to meet.

playbook_entries.guidance: From 2 to 4 PM, focus on progressing active pipeline: follow up on open opportunities, send second or third touch sequences, update opportunity stages after calls, and prepare call prep notes for tomorrow's calls. This block is not for cold outreach — that's the morning. The afternoon is for warm pipeline management. End each afternoon by reviewing your task queue and clearing tomorrow's send list.
examples: Review all opportunities in 'Contacted' stage — any that haven't moved in 5+ days get a follow-up today.
Pull tomorrow's call list and spend 10 minutes per call reviewing the account's homepage issues and the sequence history.
Sequence step 3 for accounts that replied to step 1 but haven't booked — send asset + low-friction CTA.
edge_cases: If you have calls from 2–4 PM, move afternoon cultivation to 4:30–5:30 PM.
Don't mix cold outreach into the afternoon block — message quality drops when switching context between cold and warm.
Friday afternoons: weekly review replaces cultivation block. Catch up on cultivation Saturday morning if needed.

playbook_entries.rule_title: One Specific Signal Per First Touch — Never Generic
playbook_entries.guidance: Every first-touch email must be anchored to one specific, observed signal from the prospect's website or digital presence. Not 'your design could be better' — but 'your hero describes what you do, not why a visitor should care right now, which typically costs conversion rate on cold traffic.' The signal must be: (1) specific to that company, (2) tied to a business consequence, and (3) framed as an observation, not a pitch. The first touch never asks for anything beyond permission to share more.
examples: Good opening: 'Noticed {{company_name}}'s homepage leads with service description before establishing the problem for the visitor — that structure usually means cold traffic leaves before they see your proof section.'
Good opening: 'Your CTA says Get Started but doesn't tell the visitor what they're starting or what they get — that friction typically suppresses form completions 20–30%.'
Subject line formula: 'One thing on {{company_name}}'s [page]' or '{{company_name}} — [specific issue]'
edge_cases: If you can't find a specific issue, don't send — a generic first touch is worse than no touch because it signals you haven't done the work.
Complimenting before criticising ('Great site, but...') weakens the signal — lead with the observation directly.
For agencies: frame the signal in terms of what it costs their clients, not what it costs them.

playbook_entries.rule_title: Three-Touch Minimum, Five-Touch Maximum Before Rotation
playbook_entries.guidance: Every sequence runs a minimum of 3 touches and maximum of 5 touches before rotating the prospect to a 90-day hold. Each touch must use a different angle — don't repeat the same observation twice. Touch 1: specific signal. Touch 2: different angle (revenue loss, operational cost, or competitive gap). Touch 3: asset offer. Touch 4 (if no reply): direct question with low friction. Touch 5: close the loop — acknowledge they may not be the right fit right now and offer to reconnect in 90 days. Never chase beyond touch 5.
examples: Touch sequence: 1) Specific issue + business consequence. 2) Revenue angle. 3) Asset offer. 4) Direct question ('Is this something you're working on?'). 5) Close-the-loop ('I'll leave it here — if timing changes in a quarter, happy to pick this back up').
Minimum gap between touches: 2–3 days. Maximum gap: 5 days. Don't go dark for a week and reappear.
LinkedIn is a different channel — a LinkedIn connection request counts as touch 2 in a multi-channel sequence.
edge_cases: If a prospect replies negatively at touch 2, close the loop immediately — don't continue the sequence.
For agency ICP, compress to 4 touches — agencies are time-poor and a 5-touch sequence can feel excessive.
If they open all emails but never reply, escalate to a direct LinkedIn message on touch 4.

playbook_entries.rule_title: Hold Assets Until Reply Signal — Don't Lead With Them
playbook_entries.guidance: Assets (checklist, analyser, brief builder, calculator) are not sent in the first touch. They are held until: (1) a reply is received, or (2) touch 3. Leading with an asset before establishing the specific signal dilutes the credibility of both the asset and the outreach. When an asset is sent, it must be framed as relevant to the specific issue identified — not as a generic freebie. Always pair an asset with a soft CTA that references the issue.
examples: Wrong: 'Hi, I made this homepage checklist — thought you might find it useful.' (No signal, no relevance).
Right: 'Based on what I noticed about your above-fold structure, this checklist has a specific section on clarity gaps that maps directly to the issue I described.'
Calculator send timing: only after a founder or agency acknowledges the revenue angle. Calculator before acknowledgment feels premature.
edge_cases: If a prospect replies and explicitly asks for a resource, send it immediately regardless of which touch you're on.
Never attach files — always use a link. Attachments trigger spam filters and reduce deliverability.
Brief builder is only sent after a call is booked or a prospect is in active evaluation — not in cold sequences.

playbook_entries.rule_title: Call Prep: Review Homepage Issues 15 Minutes Before Every Call
playbook_entries.guidance: Before every call, spend 15 minutes reviewing: (1) the specific issue codes identified for this account, (2) the sequence history and what was said in each touch, (3) any replies or signals in the inbox, and (4) the prospect's homepage to check for any changes since the last audit. Open the call with the specific observation that got them on the call — not with generic small talk or company background. The goal of the first 3 minutes is to confirm the pain, not to pitch.
examples: Opening: 'Thanks for making time. I wanted to start with what I noticed on the site — specifically the [issue]. Can you tell me what's driving that? Is it a resource thing, a priority thing, or something else?'
Don't open with: 'Can you tell me a bit about your company?' — this signals you haven't done the homework.
Confirm which issue resonated most in the outreach (that's the pain they're willing to discuss). Probe: what's the business impact going unresolved? Then qualify: budget, timeline, decision-maker.
edge_cases: If the prospect booked but you have limited sequence history (e.g., they replied to touch 1), still review the homepage — don't assume you know the full picture.
If the prospect cancels last-minute and reschedules, redo the 15-minute prep on the day of the rescheduled call.
International calls: check timezone 24 hours in advance. A missed call due to timezone confusion destroys trust.

playbook_entries.rule_title: Reframe Objections to Business Consequence — Never Defend
playbook_entries.guidance: When a prospect raises an objection, never defend your service or justify your existence. Instead, acknowledge the objection and reframe to the business consequence of the problem going unresolved. The reframe structure is: 'I hear that [objection]. The reason I flagged it is [specific consequence]. What happens to [metric] if that stays unresolved for another quarter?' Most objections ('too busy', 'happy with current setup', 'not the right time') are about priority, not about the problem not existing. Your job is to move it up the priority stack by making the cost of inaction visible.
examples: Objection: 'We're happy with our current design.' Reframe: 'That makes sense — and you may be. What I was flagging was the conversion rate gap specifically on paid traffic, not the design aesthetics. Those are separate problems.'

Objection: 'Not the right time.' Reframe: 'Totally understand. Is the timing about budget, internal capacity, or something else? The issue usually compounds — paid traffic keeps running while the conversion gap stays.'

Objection: 'We handle this in-house.' Reframe: 'Good to know — is the in-house team focused on this specifically, or is it one of ten priorities? The teams we work with usually have design capability but not the conversion-specific methodology.'
edge_cases: If a prospect says 'not interested' clearly and firmly twice, respect it — log and rotate to 90-day hold.
Never discount in response to price objections on a first call — you haven't established value yet. Reframe to value first.
Objections in email vs. on a call need different handling — email objections get a one-line reframe, call objections get a full probe.

playbook_entries.rule_title: Weekly Review: Measure, Log, Adjust — Every Friday
playbook_entries.guidance: Every Friday, run a 30-minute weekly review covering: (1) emails sent this week vs. target, (2) open rate by subject line format, (3) reply rate by angle, (4) assets sent vs. assets engaged, (5) calls booked vs. calls held, (6) opportunities moved forward this week, and (7) one hypothesis for next week's experiment. Log this in the Experiments section. If open rate dropped, test a new subject format next week. If reply rate is flat, rotate the angle. If assets aren't getting engagement, check the framing — not the asset itself.
examples: Weekly review format: 'This week: 45 sent, 18 opens (40%), 4 replies (9%), 1 call booked. Asset click-through: 2/4 (50%). Best-performing subject: [Company] — conversion opportunity. Experiment next week: test specificity in subject line vs. open-question format.'
If reply rate is below 5%, the angle is the problem — not the volume. Rotate angle before increasing sends.
If call-to-close rate drops, the problem is in qualification — review your call structure and objection handling.
edge_cases: If you missed a Friday review, do it Monday morning before any sends — don't skip it because it's late.
Weekly review data should feed directly into the Experiments log — a review without a next-week hypothesis is incomplete.
Public holidays: move the review to Thursday.

playbook_entries.rule_title: Single Variable Testing — 20+ Samples Per Variant
playbook_entries.guidance: Every experiment must test exactly one variable. Not 'let's try a completely different approach' — but 'Hypothesis: subject line format [Company] — [specific issue] outperforms [Quick question about Company]. Testing with 20 sends per variant this week.' When you have a winner, test a variation of the winner — not a completely new approach. Document: the variable, the hypothesis, sample size, variant A result, variant B result, winner, and next experiment. Share wins with the team immediately.
examples: Week 1: Test subject line format. A: 'One thing on {{company_name}}'s homepage' vs B: '{{company_name}} — conversion opportunity'. Measure: open rate. Min sample: 20 per variant.
Week 2 (based on week 1 winner): Test opening angle. A: message clarity gap vs B: revenue opportunity angle. Measure: reply rate.
Week 3: Test asset format. A: checklist vs B: analyser. Measure: click-through rate after asset is sent.
edge_cases: If you can't get 20 sends per variant in a single week, extend the test to two weeks — don't conclude from small samples.
Seasonal effects (August, December) can distort results — flag experiments run in these periods as seasonally influenced.
If both variants perform the same, the variable tested may not be the bottleneck — look upstream at list quality, ICP fit, or timing.
